   241→TASK: F011 - Dev Bridge (Browser-to-Native RPC)
   242→================================================================================
   243→
   244→## Problem Statement
   245→
   246→Tauri's `invoke()` function only works inside the native webview context
   247→(when the app runs via `bun tauri dev` and opens in the Tauri window).
   248→
   249→When testing via Playwright MCP, the browser loads `localhost:5173` directly,
   250→which is the Vite dev server WITHOUT Tauri's native layer. This means:
   251→
   252→### What DOES NOT work in browser mode:
   253→- `@tauri-apps/api/core` invoke() - TypeError: Cannot read 'invoke'
   254→- PTY process spawning (pty_spawn, pty_write, pty_kill)
   255→- File system operations (read/write project files)
   256→- Native dialogs (file picker, save dialog)
   257→- System notifications
   258→- Any Rust backend functionality
   259→
   260→### What DOES work in browser mode:
   261→- React components and UI rendering
   262→- State management (@zazcart/tools)
   263→- Mock data and simulated interactions
   264→- PatternFly components
   265→- Pure frontend logic
   266→
   267→## Why This Matters
   268→
   269→1. **Testing Gap**: Cannot verify PTY, file watching, or native features via Playwright
   270→2. **Development Friction**: Must manually test in Tauri window for native features
   271→3. **CI/CD Limitation**: Automated tests cannot cover critical functionality
   272→
   273→## Proposed Solution: Dev Bridge
   274→
   275→Create a development-only RPC bridge that:
   276→
   277→```
   278→┌─────────────────┐     WebSocket/HTTP      ┌─────────────────┐
   279→│  Browser        │ ◄─────────────────────► │  Rust Dev       │
   280→│  (Playwright)   │     localhost:9876      │  Server         │
   281→│                 │     + auth token        │                 │
   282→│  TypeScript     │                         │  Mirrors Tauri  │
   283→│  RPC Client     │                         │  Commands       │
   284→└─────────────────┘                         └─────────────────┘
   285→```
   286→
   287→### Architecture
   288→
   289→1. **Rust Dev Server** (only compiled in debug mode):
   290→   - Binds to localhost:9876 (or configurable port)
   291→   - Accepts WebSocket connections with token auth
   292→   - Exposes same commands as Tauri invoke (pty_spawn, etc.)
   293→   - Streams events back to client (pty-output, pty-exit)
   294→
   295→2. **TypeScript Client** (unified API):
   296→   ```typescript
   297→   // Auto-detects context and routes appropriately
   298→   async function invokeCommand(cmd: string, args: object) {
   299→     if (isTauri()) {
   300→       return await invoke(cmd, args);  // Native path
   301→     } else if (isDevBridge()) {
   302→       return await rpcClient.call(cmd, args);  // Bridge path
   303→     } else {
   304→       throw new Error('No backend available');
   305→     }
   306→   }
   307→   ```
   308→
   309→3. **Security Measures**:
   310→   - localhost-only binding (127.0.0.1, not 0.0.0.0)
   311→   - Token generated at startup, passed via env/file
   312→   - Disabled entirely in release builds (#[cfg(debug_assertions)])
   313→   - Rate limiting to prevent abuse
   314→   - Command allowlist (no arbitrary execution)
   315→
   316→### Future SDK Consideration
   317→
   318→The RPC pattern established here can later be extended for plugins:
   319→- Plugins run in sandboxed context (iframe, worker, or separate process)
   320→- Communicate with host via same RPC protocol
   321→- Host controls which commands plugins can invoke
   322→- No need to redesign later; just add permission layer
   323→
   324→**IMPORTANT**: Do NOT over-engineer for plugins now. Just ensure the bridge
   325→design doesn't preclude adding a permission/sandbox layer later.
   326→
   327→## Implementation Steps
   328→
   329→See F011 steps in features.json. Estimated complexity: Medium-High.
   330→
   331→================================================================================

# HQ - Harness Orchestrator & Dashboard
# Session Progress Log

================================================================================
SESSION: 2026-01-02 - Initial Setup
================================================================================

## What was done:
- Created project structure at ~/antonio/HQ
- Wrote HQ_SPEC.md with full system specification
- Configured .claude hooks (session-start, protect-deps, quality-check)
- Set up .harness/ with progress.txt and features.json
- Initialized Tauri 2.x project with React 19
- Installed dependencies:
  - react@19.2.3, react-dom@19.2.3
  - @patternfly/react-core@6.4.0
  - @patternfly/react-icons@6.4.0
  - @patternfly/react-styles@6.4.0
  - @zazcart/tools@0.1.0
  - @tauri-apps/cli@2.9.6, @tauri-apps/api@2.9.1
- Created basic App.tsx with PatternFly components
- Created Rust backend (src-tauri/) with Tauri 2.x setup

## Key decisions:
- Stack: Tauri 2.x + React 19 + PatternFly 6
- State management: @zazcart/tools
- PTY: Rust portable-pty crate (to be added)
- File watching: Rust notify crate (to be added)

## Next steps:
- F009: Configure @zazcart/tools state machine
- F010: Verify Tauri app runs (bun tauri dev)
- F001: Implement multi-project tab management

## Notes:
- PatternFly 6 supports React 17, 18, and 19
- PatternFly has LogViewer, Chat, and Terminal components

================================================================================
SESSION: 2026-01-02 - F010 Complete + PatternFly 6 API Fix
================================================================================

## What was done:
- Fixed PatternFly 6 API issues in App.tsx:
  - Changed Text/TextContent to Content component
  - Changed Page header prop to masthead prop
  - Fixed Label color "gold" to "yellow"
- Created app icons using `bun tauri icon` command
- Verified TypeScript compiles without errors
- Verified Tauri app builds and runs successfully
- Visual testing passed with Playwright MCP

## Key learnings:
- PatternFly 6 renamed Text/TextContent to Content
- Page component uses masthead prop, not header
- Label colors: blue, teal, green, orange, purple, red, orangered, grey, yellow

## F010: PASSED ✅
- Tauri 2.x + React 19 + PatternFly 6 running
- Icons generated from SVG source

## Next steps:
- F001: Implement multi-project tab management

================================================================================
SESSION: 2026-01-02 - F009 Complete - State Management Setup
================================================================================

## What was done:
- Created src/state/store.ts with @zazcart/tools
- Defined 5 slices:
  - ui: activeTab, theme, projectOrder, sidebarCollapsed, logPanelHeight
  - projects: Record<string, Project>
  - processes: Record<string, Process>
  - logs: entries with ring buffer (max 10000), autoScroll, filter
  - features: items[], loading, error
- Defined TypeScript interfaces: Project, Feature, Process, LogEntry
- Created selectors: activeProject, projectsArray, activeProcesses, logsForProcess, featureStats
- Created src/state/index.ts for exports
- Updated App.tsx to display state info

## Key learnings:
- @zazcart/tools useMachine hook causes infinite loop with getSnapshot
- Added @ts-nocheck temporarily due to type inference issues
- Using local useState as fallback until hook issues resolved

## F009: PASSED ✅
- State machine configured with 5 slices
- Types and selectors ready
- Visual test passed

## Next steps:
- F002: Implement Kanban board view

================================================================================
SESSION: 2026-01-02 - F001 Complete - Multi-project Tab Management
================================================================================

## What was done:
- Created TabBar component (src/components/TabBar.tsx)
- Implemented tab functionality:
  - Add new project tabs via (+) button
  - Close tabs via (X) button (only visible when >1 tab)
  - Select tabs to switch between projects
  - Drag-and-drop prepared (console.log for now)
- Updated App.tsx to integrate TabBar with mock projects
- Fixed PatternFly button nesting issue (span with role="button" instead)
- Visual testing passed with Playwright MCP

## Key learnings:
- PatternFly Tab is already a button, cannot nest Button inside
- Use span with role="button" for accessible close button inside tabs
- isOverflowHorizontal prop handles tab overflow automatically

## F001: PASSED ✅
- TabBar renders project tabs
- Add/Close/Select functionality works
- Console clean (no errors)

## Next steps:
- F003: Implement real-time log panel

================================================================================
SESSION: 2026-01-02 - F002 Complete - Kanban Board View
================================================================================

## What was done:
- Created KanbanBoard component (src/components/KanbanBoard.tsx)
  - 6 columns: BACKLOG, TODO, DOING, REVIEW, QA, DONE
  - Badge with feature count per column
  - Features sorted by priority within columns
  - Horizontal scroll for responsive layout
- Created KanbanCard component (src/components/KanbanCard.tsx)
  - Expandable card with description and steps
  - Labels for priority (critical/high/medium/low)
  - Labels for category (core/ui/setup)
  - Visual icons for step completion status
- Integrated KanbanBoard into App.tsx with mock features
- Fixed PatternFly 6 API issues (removed deprecated isFlat prop)
- Visual testing passed with Playwright MCP

## Key learnings:
- PatternFly 6 Card does not have isFlat prop
- Feature status mapping: "done"/"completed"/"passed" → DONE column
- Features with passes: true always go to DONE column

## F002: PASSED ✅
- 6-column Kanban board rendering correctly
- Cards expand to show description and steps
- Console clean (no errors)

## Next steps:
- F003: Implement real-time log panel with ANSI colors

================================================================================
SESSION: 2026-01-02 - F003 Complete - Real-time Log Panel
================================================================================

## What was done:
- Installed @patternfly/react-log-viewer@6.3.0 package
- Created LogPanel component (src/components/LogPanel.tsx)
  - PatternFly LogViewer with virtual scroll
  - Native ANSI color support via LogViewer component
  - Dark theme for terminal-like appearance
  - Line numbers enabled
- Implemented toolbar features:
  - Process filter dropdown (All Processes + individual processes)
  - Badge showing log count per process
  - LogViewerSearch for text search in logs
  - Wrap text checkbox
  - Auto-scroll toggle (pause/resume)
  - Download logs button (exports as .txt)
  - Clear logs button
- Added mock data with ANSI escape sequences
- Integrated into App.tsx with mock processes and logs
- Visual testing passed with Playwright MCP

## Key learnings:
- PatternFly LogViewer handles ANSI colors natively
- ToolbarGroup variant="icon-button-group" → "action-group-plain" in PF6
- LogViewerSearch must be inside LogViewer context
- Date.toLocaleTimeString doesn't support fractionalSecondDigits

## F003: PASSED ✅
- LogPanel with virtual scroll rendering correctly
- Process filter dropdown working
- Search, auto-scroll, download, clear controls functional
- Console clean (no errors)

## Next steps:
- F004: PTY process controller (Rust backend)

================================================================================
SESSION: 2026-01-04 - F004 Complete - PTY Process Controller
================================================================================

## What was done:
- Added Rust dependencies to Cargo.toml:
  - portable-pty 0.9 (cross-platform PTY)
  - uuid 1.x (process IDs)
  - parking_lot 0.12 (mutex for process management)
- Created src-tauri/src/pty.rs module:
  - PtyManager struct with HashMap<String, PtyProcess>
  - ProcessInfo struct for frontend communication
  - PtyOutput and PtyExit event payloads
- Implemented Tauri commands:
  - pty_spawn: Create new PTY process
  - pty_write: Send keystrokes to process
  - pty_resize: Change terminal dimensions
  - pty_kill: Terminate process
  - pty_list: List all processes
  - pty_get: Get process info by ID
- Streaming output via Tauri events:
  - "pty-output" event with process ID and data
  - "pty-exit" event with process ID and exit code
- Created TerminalPanel component (src/components/TerminalPanel.tsx):
  - Tauri context detection (isTauri())
  - Dynamic import of @tauri-apps/api
  - Browser mode warning with disabled controls
  - Start Shell / Send / Kill buttons
  - Process info display

## Key learnings:
- portable-pty 0.9 is current stable version (from wezterm project)
- Tauri invoke only works in native context, not browser
- Use dynamic imports for @tauri-apps/api to avoid browser errors
- Check `__TAURI__` in window to detect native context

## F004: PASSED ✅
- Rust code compiles without errors
- TypeScript compiles without errors
- TerminalPanel shows Browser Mode warning correctly
- PTY functionality available only in native Tauri app
- Console clean (no errors)

## Next steps:
- F011: Dev Bridge (high priority - blocks Playwright testing)
- F005: Harness state visualization

================================================================================
TASK: F011 - Dev Bridge (Browser-to-Native RPC)
================================================================================

## Problem Statement

Tauri's `invoke()` function only works inside the native webview context
(when the app runs via `bun tauri dev` and opens in the Tauri window).

When testing via Playwright MCP, the browser loads `localhost:5173` directly,
which is the Vite dev server WITHOUT Tauri's native layer. This means:

### What DOES NOT work in browser mode:
- `@tauri-apps/api/core` invoke() - TypeError: Cannot read 'invoke'
- PTY process spawning (pty_spawn, pty_write, pty_kill)
- File system operations (read/write project files)
- Native dialogs (file picker, save dialog)
- System notifications
- Any Rust backend functionality

### What DOES work in browser mode:
- React components and UI rendering
- State management (@zazcart/tools)
- Mock data and simulated interactions
- PatternFly components
- Pure frontend logic

## Why This Matters

1. **Testing Gap**: Cannot verify PTY, file watching, or native features via Playwright
2. **Development Friction**: Must manually test in Tauri window for native features
3. **CI/CD Limitation**: Automated tests cannot cover critical functionality

## Proposed Solution: Dev Bridge

Create a development-only RPC bridge that:

```
┌─────────────────┐     WebSocket/HTTP      ┌─────────────────┐
│  Browser        │ ◄─────────────────────► │  Rust Dev       │
│  (Playwright)   │     localhost:9876      │  Server         │
│                 │     + auth token        │                 │
│  TypeScript     │                         │  Mirrors Tauri  │
│  RPC Client     │                         │  Commands       │
└─────────────────┘                         └─────────────────┘
```

### Architecture

1. **Rust Dev Server** (only compiled in debug mode):
   - Binds to localhost:9876 (or configurable port)
   - Accepts WebSocket connections with token auth
   - Exposes same commands as Tauri invoke (pty_spawn, etc.)
   - Streams events back to client (pty-output, pty-exit)

2. **TypeScript Client** (unified API):
   ```typescript
   // Auto-detects context and routes appropriately
   async function invokeCommand(cmd: string, args: object) {
     if (isTauri()) {
       return await invoke(cmd, args);  // Native path
     } else if (isDevBridge()) {
       return await rpcClient.call(cmd, args);  // Bridge path
     } else {
       throw new Error('No backend available');
     }
   }
   ```

3. **Security Measures**:
   - localhost-only binding (127.0.0.1, not 0.0.0.0)
   - Token generated at startup, passed via env/file
   - Disabled entirely in release builds (#[cfg(debug_assertions)])
   - Rate limiting to prevent abuse
   - Command allowlist (no arbitrary execution)

### Future SDK Consideration

The RPC pattern established here can later be extended for plugins:
- Plugins run in sandboxed context (iframe, worker, or separate process)
- Communicate with host via same RPC protocol
- Host controls which commands plugins can invoke
- No need to redesign later; just add permission layer

**IMPORTANT**: Do NOT over-engineer for plugins now. Just ensure the bridge
design doesn't preclude adding a permission/sandbox layer later.

## Implementation Steps

See F011 steps in features.json. Estimated complexity: Medium-High.

================================================================================

content.thinking: Vou adicionar a nova feature F012 para UX nativa. Primeiro passo é "Research" para descobrir todas as técnicas possíveis para deixar o app mais nativo.

input.new_string:
"notes": {
      "problem": "Tauri invoke() only works in native webview context. Playwright tests run in browser (localhost:5173) which has no access to Rust backend. This blocks testing of PTY, file system, and other native features.",
      "constraints": [
        "Must NOT introduce security vulnerabilities (no arbitrary code execution)",
        "Must only work in development mode (disabled in production builds)",
        "Should be extensible for future plugin SDK without over-engineering now"
      ],
      "approach": "Create a dev-only WebSocket/HTTP server in Rust that mirrors Tauri commands. TypeScript client auto-detects context and routes to Tauri invoke or RPC bridge. Token auth prevents unauthorized access.",
      "future_consideration": "Plugin SDK may use similar RPC pattern for sandboxed plugin communication"
    }
  },
  {
    "id": "F012",
    "description": "Native UX Polish - Make the app feel like a native macOS application, not a web app",
    "category": "ux",
    "priority": "high",
    "status": "backlog",
    "passes": false,
    "steps": [
      "RESEARCH: Study macOS native app UX patterns (Finder, Notes, Xcode) and document differences from web apps",
      "CSS: Disable focus outlines on non-keyboard navigation (use :focus-visible)",
      "CSS: Disable text selection on UI elements (labels, buttons, headers) - keep it only on content",
      "CSS: Add -webkit-app-region: drag for custom titlebar drag areas",
      "Tauri: Configure transparent window with vibrancy/blur effect (macOS Sonoma/Tahoe style)",
      "Tauri: Configure proper window decorations (trafficLightPosition, hiddenTitle)",
      "UX: Implement native-style context menus (right-click)",
      "UX: Add native keyboard shortcuts (Cmd+W close tab, Cmd+T new tab, etc.)",
      "UX: Disable browser-like behaviors (Cmd+F default, zoom gestures, etc.)",
      "ANIMATION: Add subtle transitions that match macOS feel (ease-out, spring physics)"
    ],
    "notes": {
      "research_topics": [
        "Tauri window vibrancy/transparency on macOS",
        "CSS user-select: none vs auto for native feel",
        "focus-visible vs focus for keyboard navigation",
        "-webkit-app-region for draggable areas",
        "macOS Sonoma/Tahoe design language",
        "Native context menu APIs in Tauri",
        "Preventing web behaviors (zoom, select-all, find)",
        "Spring animations for native feel"
      ],
      "known_techniques": [
        "user-select: none on non-content elements",
        ":focus-visible instead of :focus for outline",
        "-webkit-user-drag: none on images",
        "overscroll-behavior: none to prevent bounce",
        "touch-action: none where appropriate"
      ],
      "inspiration": [
        "Linear app (electron but feels native)",
        "Warp terminal",
        "Arc browser",
        "Raycast"
      ]
    }
  }
]

input.new_string:
## Implementation Steps

See F011 steps in features.json. Estimated complexity: Medium-High.

================================================================================
SESSION: 2026-01-04 - Added F012 Native UX Polish Task
================================================================================

## What was done:
- Added F012: Native UX Polish to features.json
- Priority: high
- Category: ux (new category)

## F012 Scope:
First step is RESEARCH to document all techniques for native feel:

### Known techniques to implement:
1. Focus outlines: Use :focus-visible instead of :focus
2. Text selection: Disable on UI elements (user-select: none)
3. Drag regions: -webkit-app-region: drag for titlebar
4. Window vibrancy: Tauri transparent window + blur (macOS Tahoe)
5. Window decorations: trafficLightPosition, hiddenTitle
6. Context menus: Native right-click menus via Tauri
7. Keyboard shortcuts: Cmd+W, Cmd+T, etc.
8. Disable web behaviors: Cmd+F, zoom gestures, bounce scroll
9. Animations: Spring physics, ease-out transitions

### Apps to study for inspiration:
- Linear (Electron but feels native)
- Warp terminal
- Arc browser
- Raycast

## Next steps:
- F012 Step 1: Research macOS native patterns and document findings

================================================================================

### Apps to study for inspiration:

================================================================================
TASK: F011 - Dev Bridge (Browser-to-Native RPC)
================================================================================

## Problem Statement

Tauri's `invoke()` function only works inside the native webview context
(when the app runs via `bun tauri dev` and opens in the Tauri window).

When testing via Playwright MCP, the browser loads `localhost:5173` directly,
which is the Vite dev server WITHOUT Tauri's native layer. This means:

### What DOES NOT work in browser mode:
- `@tauri-apps/api/core` invoke() - TypeError: Cannot read 'invoke'
- PTY process spawning (pty_spawn, pty_write, pty_kill)
- File system operations (read/write project files)
- Native dialogs (file picker, save dialog)
- System notifications
- Any Rust backend functionality

### What DOES work in browser mode:
- React components and UI rendering
- State management (@zazcart/tools)
- Mock data and simulated interactions
- PatternFly components
- Pure frontend logic

## Why This Matters

1. **Testing Gap**: Cannot verify PTY, file watching, or native features via Playwright
2. **Development Friction**: Must manually test in Tauri window for native features
3. **CI/CD Limitation**: Automated tests cannot cover critical functionality

## Proposed Solution: Dev Bridge

Create a development-only RPC bridge that:

```
┌─────────────────┐     WebSocket/HTTP      ┌─────────────────┐
│  Browser        │ ◄─────────────────────► │  Rust Dev       │
│  (Playwright)   │     localhost:9876      │  Server         │
│                 │     + auth token        │                 │
│  TypeScript     │                         │  Mirrors Tauri  │
│  RPC Client     │                         │  Commands       │
└─────────────────┘                         └─────────────────┘
```

### Architecture

1. **Rust Dev Server** (only compiled in debug mode):
   - Binds to localhost:9876 (or configurable port)
   - Accepts WebSocket connections with token auth
   - Exposes same commands as Tauri invoke (pty_spawn, etc.)
   - Streams events back to client (pty-output, pty-exit)

2. **TypeScript Client** (unified API):
   ```typescript
   // Auto-detects context and routes appropriately
   async function invokeCommand(cmd: string, args: object) {
     if (isTauri()) {
       return await invoke(cmd, args);  // Native path
     } else if (isDevBridge()) {
       return await rpcClient.call(cmd, args);  // Bridge path
     } else {
       throw new Error('No backend available');
     }
   }
   ```

3. **Security Measures**:
   - localhost-only binding (127.0.0.1, not 0.0.0.0)
   - Token generated at startup, passed via env/file
   - Disabled entirely in release builds (#[cfg(debug_assertions)])
   - Rate limiting to prevent abuse
   - Command allowlist (no arbitrary execution)

### Future SDK Consideration

The RPC pattern established here can later be extended for plugins:
- Plugins run in sandboxed context (iframe, worker, or separate process)
- Communicate with host via same RPC protocol
- Host controls which commands plugins can invoke
- No need to redesign later; just add permission layer

**IMPORTANT**: Do NOT over-engineer for plugins now. Just ensure the bridge
design doesn't preclude adding a permission/sandbox layer later.

## Implementation Steps

See F011 steps in features.json. Estimated complexity: Medium-High.

================================================================================

Here are the search results for Tauri 2.x window vibrancy, transparency, and blur on macOS:

## Key Findings

### Official `window-vibrancy` Crate
If you're using tauri@v1, you need to use version 0.4 of this crate. This implies that newer versions work with Tauri v2.

For macOS, you use `apply_vibrancy(&window, NSVisualEffectMaterial::HudWindow, None, None)` - the `apply_vibrancy` function is only supported on macOS.(no content)On Linux, blur and vibrancy effects are unsupported as they are controlled by the compositor installed on the end-user system.

### Tauri v2 Official Documentation
Tauri provides lots of options for customizing the look and feel of your app's window. You can create custom titlebars, have transparent windows, enforce size constraints, and more.

### Configuration Requirements
To enable vibrancy, you need to: set `html, body { background: transparent }`, set `"windows": [ { "transparent": true } ]` in tauri.conf.json, and on macOS set `"macOSPrivateApi": true`.

### Known Bug (February 2025)
When transparency is enabled in Tauri, the backdrop fails to function correctly and displays unexpected behavior. This issue is more noticeable when the component with backdrop-filter is applied.(no content)The backdrop-filter works if you disable transparency or add a background to the parent element. Additionally, in previous versions on macOS Sonoma, it works as expected.(no content)This was reported on Mac OS 15.3.1 with Tauri 2.2.5, wry 0.48.1, and tao 0.31.1.

### Note on Tauri v2 Integration
The window-vibrancy crate notes indicate it was "added to Tauri in v2" - meaning some of this functionality may now be built into Tauri v2 directly.

Support tables for HTML5, CSS3, etc","url":"https://caniuse.com/css-focus-visible"},{"title":":focus-visible in WebKit - May 2021","url":"https://blogs.igalia.com/mrego/2021/06/07/focus-visible-in-webkit-may-2021/"},{"title":"Building Native-like Experiences on the Web with PWAs | SpiceFactory","url":"https://spicefactory.co/blog/2019/10/18/native-like-pwas/"},{"title":"Managing User Focus with :focus-visible | CSS-Tricks","url":"https://css-tricks.com/managing-user-focus-with-focus-visible/"},{"title":":focus-visible Is Here · Matthias Ott","url":"https://matthiasott.com/notes/focus-visible-is-here"},{"title":"Display content in the title bar area using Window Controls Overlay - Microsoft Edge Developer documentation | Microsoft Learn","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/microsoft-edge/progressive-web-apps/how-to/window-controls-overlay"}]

Here are the search results for CSS techniques to achieve a native desktop app feel:

## Key Findings

### `:focus-visible` Pseudo-class
The `:focus-visible` pseudo-class applies while an element matches the `:focus` pseudo-class and the UA (User Agent) determines via heuristics that the focus should be made evident on the element.(no content)Many browsers show a "focus ring" by default in this case.(no content)The `:focus-visible` pseudo-class matches elements based on the browser's heuristics. It allows web authors to style the focus indicator only if it would be drawn natively.

### `-webkit-app-region` for Draggable Areas (Electron)
To implement draggable areas, you can use two CSS classes: `.drag { -webkit-app-region: drag; }` and `.no-drag { -webkit-app-region: no-drag; }`. You can add a `.drag` class to the entire titlebar container element and selectively add `.no-drag` to elements that should prevent the drag interaction.

### `app-region` for PWAs (Window Controls Overlay)
You can use the `app-region` CSS property to offer more ways for users to drag the app. For example, if your app has its own titlebar, you can turn its titlebar into a window drag handle using `app-region: drag;`.(no content)A Progressive Web App (PWA) installed on the desktop can display content where the title bar normally is, to make the PWA feel more native, by using the Window Controls Overlay API. This API allows you to display web content over the entire surface area of the app, moves the critical system-required window controls into an overlay, and makes it possible for your content to stay clear of this overlay.

### `user-select` for Native Feel
Native apps do not provide text selection as web browsers do, e.g. button labels should not be selectable, etc. The `-webkit-user-select` property allows control of the selection behaviour. You probably want this only on mobile, so put it under a media query.

### Additional Electron Tips for Native Feel
Tailwind sets a default font size of 16px. This looks fine on the web, but it certainly stands out inside a desktop app. A default system font size in native apps is 13px, but 14px can work as a compromise.(no content)When an Electron app loses focus, replacing colors with shades of gray (like dimming a selected navigation item) is a small pattern from native apps that is easy to miss but helps the app feel more native.

This API allows you to display web content over the entire surface area of the app, moves the critical system-required window controls into an overlay, and makes it possible for your content to stay clear of this overlay.

### `user-select` for Native Feel
Native apps do not provide text selection as web browsers do, e.g. button labels should not be selectable, etc. The `-webkit-user-select` property allows control of the selection behaviour. You probably want this only on mobile, so put it under a media query.

### Additional Electron Tips for Native Feel
Tailwind sets a default font size of 16px. This looks fine on the web, but it certainly stands out inside a desktop app. A default system font size in native apps is 13px, but 14px can work as a compromise.(no content)When an Electron app loses focus, replacing colors with shades of gray (like dimming a selected navigation item) is a small pattern from native apps that is easy to miss but helps the app feel more native.

Perfect. Now I have a comprehensive understanding of the codebase. Let me create a detailed report based on my findings.

## HQ Codebase Exploration Report: Tauri Configuration, Styling, and UX

Based on my thorough examination of the HQ codebase, here's the current state and what would need modification for native UX polish:

---

## 1. TAURI WINDOW CONFIGURATION

### Current State (`src-tauri/tauri.conf.json`)

**Window Settings:**
```json
{
  "windows": [{
    "title": "HQ - Harness Orchestrator",
    "width": 1280,
    "height": 800,
    "minWidth": 800,
    "minHeight": 600,
    "resizable": true,
    "fullscreen": false
  }]
}
```

**Key Observations:**
- Window is configured with sensible defaults (1280x800 resolution with min constraints)
- No decorations customization (uses platform-default title bar)
- No vibrancy, transparency, or advanced window features enabled
- CSP is disabled (`"csp": null`)

### Rust Backend (`src-tauri/src/lib.rs`)

**Current State:**
- Minimal window initialization
- No window event handlers or lifecycle management
- No title bar customization
- No focus/blur handlers for app-level state changes
- Uses standard Tauri Builder pattern with default window handling

**Missing for Native UX Polish:**
- Window resize/move event listeners
- Focus/blur event handlers (to pause processes, update UI state)
- Window maximize/minimize/restore handlers
- Custom title bar with platform-specific styling
- macOS-specific features (traffic light buttons, vibrancy)
- Keyboard shortcut global registration
- Window position/size persistence

---

## 2. CSS & STYLING APPROACH

### Current Styling Files

**`src/index.css`** (Global styles - minimal):
```css
:root {
  font-family: var(--pf-t--global--font--family--body);
}

* {
  box-sizing: border-box;
  margin: 0;
  padding: 0;
}

body {
  min-height: 100vh;
  background-color: var(--pf-t--global--background--color--primary--default);
}

#root {
  min-height: 100vh;
}
```

**Styling Approach:**
- Entirely PatternFly 6 token-based (CSS custom properties: `--pf-t--*`)
- No custom CSS beyond minimal reset
- No global theme management
- No dark mode implementation beyond PatternFly defaults
- No Tailwind or other CSS framework

### PatternFly Theme Integration

**`src/main.tsx`:**
```typescript
import '@patternfly/react-core/dist/styles/base.css';
```

**Theme Variables Used Across Components:**
- `--pf-t--global--background--color--primary--default` (page background)
- `--pf-t--global--background--color--secondary--default` (component backgrounds)
- `--pf-t--global--border--color--default` (borders)
- `--pf-t--global--font--family--body` (typography)

### Inline Styles in Components

**Examples from examination:**
- `TerminalPanel.tsx`: Hardcoded dark theme (`backgroundColor: "#1e1e1e"`, `color: "#d4d4d4"`)
- `KanbanBoard.tsx`: Hardcoded light theme (`backgroundColor: '#f0f0f0'`, `backgroundColor: '#fafafa'`)
- `TabBar.tsx`: Uses only PatternFly tokens
- `LogPanel.tsx`: Uses PatternFly LogViewer with `theme="dark"` prop

**Problem:** Inconsistent theming—some components hardcoded colors that don't respect PatternFly theme tokens.

---

## 3. EXISTING UX-RELATED CODE

### Keyboard Handling

**Found in Components:**

1. **TabBar.tsx** (tab close):
   ```typescript
   onKeyDown={(e) => {
     if (e.key === 'Enter' || e.key === ' ') {
       handleClose(...);
     }
   }}
   ```
   - Only handles Enter/Space for close button
   - No application-level keyboard shortcuts
   - No global hotkey management

2. **TerminalPanel.tsx**:
   ```typescript
   handleKeyDown = (event: React.KeyboardEvent) => {
     if (event.key === 'Enter') {
       handleSend();
     }
   }
   ```
   - Only handles Enter in terminal input
   - No cmd/ctrl modifiers
   - No keyboard navigation for terminal history

### Focus Management

**TabBar.tsx:**
```typescript
useEffect(() => {
  if (tabsRef.current && activeProjectId) {
    const activeTab = tabsRef.current.querySelector(`[data-tab-id="${activeProjectId}"]`);
    if (activeTab instanceof HTMLElement) {
      activeTab.focus();
    }
  }
}, [activeProjectId]);
```

**Observations:**
- Basic focus management after tab switches
- No focus trap/management for modals
- No focus restoration
- No ARIA live regions

### Context Menus

**Status:** NONE FOUND
- No right-click context menus anywhere
- No macOS "secondary click" support
- No platform-specific menu integration with Tauri

### Drag & Drop

**TabBar.tsx:**
```typescript
const handleDragStart = (e: React.DragEvent, projectId: string) => {
  setDraggedId(projectId);
  e.dataTransfer.effectAllowed = 'move';
  e.dataTransfer.setData('text/plain', projectId);
};
```

**Observations:**
- Tab reordering drag-drop exists but **not fully connected to state**
- Only visual feedback (opacity change)
- No event reordering implementation

### Auto-scroll

**LogPanel.tsx:**
```typescript
useEffect(() => {
  if (isAutoScroll && logViewerRef.current) {
    logViewerRef.current.scrollToBottom();
  }
}, [logData.length, isAutoScroll]);
```

**Observations:**
- Auto-scroll with toggle
- Manual scroll detection to disable auto-scroll
- Good UX pattern, but only in LogPanel

---

## 4. STATE MANAGEMENT

### Current Approach

**Files:**
- `src/state/store.ts` - Type definitions (not examined in detail but referenced)
- `src/state/index.ts` - Export file

**Observations from App.tsx:**
- Uses React `useState` hooks for all state (projects, activeProjectId, theme, logs)
- **Violates CLAUDE.md rule:** Should use `@zazcart/tools` state-machine instead
- Theme state exists but no persistence
- No global keyboard shortcut state

---

## WHAT NEEDS MODIFICATION FOR NATIVE UX POLISH

### High Priority (Functionality)

| Item | Current | Needed |
|------|---------|--------|
| Global keyboard shortcuts | None | Cmd+N (new), Cmd+T (new tab), Cmd+W (close), Cmd+Tab (switch), etc. |
| Context menus | None | Right-click menus for tabs, cards, log entries |
| Theme persistence | State-only | Save to disk, restore on app launch |
| Window event handling | None | Focus/blur, resize, move lifecycle |
| Tab drag-drop reorder | UI only | Complete state integration |
| Keyboard navigation | Minimal | Full keyboard access to all features |

### Medium Priority (Polish)

| Item | Current | Needed |
|------|---------|--------|
| Vibrancy/transparency | None | macOS: vibrancy effects on sidebar |
| Custom title bar | Platform default | Integrate app controls into title bar |
| Window position persistence | None | Remember size/position on close |
| Dark mode detection | Manual toggle | System preference detection (macOS/Windows) |
| Haptic feedback | None | Subtle haptics for actions (macOS) |
| Activity indicators | None | Spinning icon in dock/taskbar during process run |
| App menu | None | File menu, Edit menu with standard items |

### Low Priority (Enhancement)

| Item | Current | Needed |
|------|---------|--------|
| Smooth animations | Basic transitions | Optimized spring animations |
| Accessibility | PatternFly default | Enhanced screen reader support |
| High-DPI scaling | OS default | Explicit scaling for HiDPI displays |
| Color consistency | Mixed (hardcoded + tokens) | Unified token-based theming |

---

## KEY FINDINGS

### What's Good

1. **Foundation is solid:** PatternFly 6 provides professional enterprise UI
2. **Focus handling exists:** TabBar has focus management pattern
3. **Event listeners in place:** useEffect cleanup properly handles listeners
4. **Drag-drop structure:** UI scaffolding for tab reordering exists
5. **Process control:** Tauri backend PTY integration is functional

### What's Missing

1. **No global keyboard bindings** - Critical for desktop app
2. **No context menus** - Expected in native apps
3. **No window lifecycle** - App doesn't respond to window events
4. **Theme inconsistency** - Mix of hardcoded colors and tokens
5. **No theme persistence** - Manual toggle each session
6. **State management violation** - Using React hooks instead of @zazcart/tools
7. **No right-click integration** - Only left-click interactions supported

---

## RECOMMENDED IMPLEMENTATION ORDER

1. **Fix state management** (migrate to @zazcart/tools per CLAUDE.md)
2. **Implement global keyboard shortcuts** (Tauri key listener)
3. **Add window event handlers** (focus/blur/resize/move)
4. **Unify theme with CSS variables** (remove hardcoded colors)
5. **Implement context menus** (right-click handlers + menu building)
6. **Complete tab reorder** (connect drag-drop to state)
7. **Add theme persistence** (localStorage or Tauri config file)
8. **Platform-specific polish** (vibrancy, dock integration, app menu)

---

## FILES TO MODIFY

**Tauri Backend:**
- `/Users/anotonio.silva/antonio/HQ/src-tauri/src/lib.rs` - Window initialization
- `/Users/anotonio.silva/antonio/HQ/src-tauri/tauri.conf.json` - Window config

**Frontend:**
- `/Users/anotonio.silva/antonio/HQ/src/App.tsx` - State migration, theme logic
- `/Users/anotonio.silva/antonio/HQ/src/index.css` - Global CSS with theme variables
- `/Users/anotonio.silva/antonio/HQ/src/components/*.tsx` - Remove inline color styles
- `/Users/anotonio.silva/antonio/HQ/src/main.tsx` - Add theme provider if needed
- `/Users/anotonio.silva/antonio/HQ/vite.config.ts` - No changes needed (already minimal)

---

This exploration shows a **solid foundation with 60% of the infrastructure in place**, but requiring **focused work on native UX integration** (keyboard, menus, window events) and **state management correctness** to meet professional desktop app standards.

content.content:
# Window Customization Information from Tauri Documentation

Based on the provided content, here's what's documented:

## Available Configuration Options

**Decorations:**
The page mentions `"decorations": false` in `tauri.conf.json` to remove window decorations for custom titlebar implementation.

**Configuration Methods:**
Three approaches are listed: "Through tauri.conf.json, Through the JavaScript API, [and] Through the Window in Rust."

## Custom Titlebar Implementation

The documentation provides a complete example including:
- CSS styling for a titlebar positioned at the top
- HTML structure with minimize, maximize, and close buttons
- JavaScript code using `getCurrentWindow()` to control window actions

**Key note on drag regions:** "data-tauri-drag-region will only work on the element to which it is directly applied."

## macOS-Specific Example

A transparent titlebar approach is demonstrated using:
- `TitleBarStyle::Transparent` in Rust
- The `cocoa` crate to access native macOS APIs
- Setting background color via `NSColor` and `setBackgroundColor_`

## Not Covered

The provided content does **not** include information about:
- Vibrancy or blur effects
- Traffic light positioning
- Hidden title options
- trafficLightPosition configuration

These features may exist in Tauri but aren't documented in this particular page excerpt.

content.content:
# window-vibrancy Setup for Tauri 2.x

Based on the GitHub repository content, here's what I can provide:

## 1. Adding to Cargo.toml

The page doesn't specify the exact Cargo.toml syntax, but references the crate at [crates.io/crates/window-vibrancy](https://crates.io/crates/window-vibrancy). For Tauri 2.x, add a dependency line with the latest version (currently 0.7.1).

## 2. Rust Code Example

```rust
use window_vibrancy::{apply_vibrancy, NSVisualEffectMaterial};

#[cfg(target_os = "macos")]
apply_vibrancy(
    &window, 
    NSVisualEffectMaterial::HudWindow, 
    None, 
    None
).expect("Unsupported platform! 'apply_vibrancy' is only supported on macOS");
```

## 3. NSVisualEffectMaterial Options

The documentation mentions `NSVisualEffectMaterial::HudWindow` in the example but doesn't list all available options in the provided content.

## 4. Tauri 2.x Compatibility

"If you're using `tauri@v1`, you need to use version `0.4` of this crate." This implies version 0.7.1+ supports Tauri 2.x, though explicit version requirements aren't detailed.

## 5. Setup Requirements

For Tauri integration, configure:
- CSS: `html, body { background: transparent }`
- Config: `"windows": [ { "transparent": true } ]`
- macOS only: `"macOSPrivateApi": true`

See the `examples/tauri` directory in the repository for complete implementation details.

- Config: `"windows": [ { "transparent": true } ]`
- macOS only: `"macOSPrivateApi": true`

Based on the search results, here's what I found about NSVisualEffectView and its materials:

## NSVisualEffectView Overview

NSVisualEffectView is a view that adds translucency and vibrancy effects to the views in your interface.(no content)The Material constants specify the material shown by the visual effect view.

## Material Categories

In earlier versions of macOS, the materials available in NSVisualEffectView belonged to two very different categories: one was materials defined by their color or brightness, like light, dark, mediumLight, and ultraDark - all variants of the blurry translucent background used in app sidebars, differing only in what shade of gray they used. The other was materials defined by where they should be used: titlebar, selection, menu, popover, sidebar.(no content)The 10.14 SDK cleaned this all up.(no content)Available materials include ExtraLight, Light, and Dark. Compared to UIKit it also has some desktop-specific materials, for example Titlebar, Sidebar, or Popover.

## Usage with fullSizeContentView

To use NSVisualEffectView with a transparent titlebar and fullSizeContentView, you can set up the visual effect view with blending mode `.behindWindow`, state `.active`, and then insert `.fullSizeContentView` into the window's styleMask while setting `titlebarAppearsTransparent = true`.(no content)You can set NSVisualEffectView as contentView of NSWindow, and your main view as subview of it.

The search results don't provide a complete list of all NSVisualEffectMaterial options specifically for macOS Sonoma or Sequoia. Would you like me to search more specifically for the complete enumeration of material values in the latest macOS versions?

Based on the search results, here's what I found about disabling browser shortcuts, context menu, zoom, and pinch in Tauri 2:

## tauri-plugin-prevent-default

The most comprehensive solution is the **tauri-plugin-prevent-default** plugin. This plugin disables default browser shortcuts in your Tauri app, such as F3 or Ctrl+J.

### Installation
Install by adding to your Cargo.toml: `tauri-plugin-prevent-default = "2.1"`. Then use it with `tauri::Builder::default().plugin(tauri_plugin_prevent_default::init())`. By default, it will disable all shortcuts.

### Selective Disabling
You can use flags to determine which shortcuts the plugin should disable. By default, it will disable all of them.

Example for specific flags:
```rust
use tauri_plugin_prevent_default::Flags;
let prevent = tauri_plugin_prevent_default::Builder::new()
    .with_flags(Flags::CONTEXT_MENU | Flags::PRINT | Flags::DOWNLOADS)
    .build();
```

If you want to keep only CONTEXT_MENU, DEV_TOOLS, and RELOAD enabled in dev mode, you can build the plugin with `tauri_plugin_prevent_default::debug` instead.

## Pinch-to-Zoom Issues

### Windows (WebView2)
There is an environment variable that can be used: `WEBVIEW2_ADDITIONAL_BROWSER_ARGUMENTS` with arguments `--disable-pinch`.(no content)WRY added an option to enable/disable zoom shortcuts for WebView2, disabled by default.

### Linux (WebKitGTK)
Developers have reported that pinch-to-zoom scales the entire page on Linux, but not on Windows, and need a way to completely disable this behavior.(no content)There's a feature request to provide an API or configuration option in Tauri (or WebKitGTK via wry) to disable pinch-to-zoom page scaling on Linux. Meta tags, CSS, touch event listeners, and setting `webview.set_zoom_level(1.0)` via Rust don't fully disable it.(no content)On Linux, the zoom is directly handled by GTK and completely ignores any in-browser feature. This is currently an upstream limitation.

### macOS (WKWebView)
For WKWebView (the webkitgtk equivalent on macOS), some have tried specifying a maximum-scale on the meta HTML element.

Here are the search results for CSS spring animations with a native feel in Framer Motion and React for 2025:

## Motion (formerly Framer Motion)

"Real spring physics for great-feeling animations" is a core feature of Motion. It provides hover, press and drag gestures that feel native, not "webby".(no content)Motion's unique hybrid engine combines the performance of native browser animations with the flexibility of JavaScript.(no content)Motion leverages the same high-performance browser animations as pure CSS, ensuring your UIs stay smooth and snappy.

### Spring Animation Defaults
Physical properties like `x` or `scale` are animated with spring physics by default, whereas values like `opacity` or `color` are animated with duration-based easing curves.

### useSpring Hook
Motion for React's `useSpring` hook creates smooth spring animations for motion values, allowing you to animate with `.set()`, track values, and customize transitions.

## 2025 Updates

Framer Motion released v11 in 2025, introducing improved layout animations with more reliable handling of complex layout transitions (especially in React 19 projects with concurrent rendering), as well as performance enhancements for large numbers of animated elements.(no content)Framer Motion 12.23.6 introduces automatic reduced-motion fallbacks and scroll-linked timelines, while React Spring 10.0.1 refactored its physics core for 30% smaller bundles.

## Motion vs React Spring Comparison

Motion's prop syntax (`<motion.div animate={{x:100}} />`) feels like CSS-in-JS, while Spring relies on hooks returning animated values (`useSpring`). Motion groups multiple states (hover, tap) under one key via Variants. Motion automatically animates position changes for Layout Animations, whereas Spring needs manual measurement.(no content)Gesture + spring delivers native-feeling throw & settle.(no content)Framer Motion is the best all-rounder for rich, interactive experiences, while React Spring is ideal for smooth, physics-driven animations. For most modern React apps, Framer Motion strikes the best balance between expressiveness and developer ergonomics.

## Why Spring Animations Feel Native

Framer Motion's interactions are physics-based, not fake transitions. That's why animations feel natural instead of robotic.(no content)Framer Motion is a production-ready animation library for React that lets you animate components using simple props instead of complex CSS keyframes or imperative JavaScript.

Here are the search results for Tauri 2 global keyboard shortcuts and accelerator menu:

## Tauri 2 Global Shortcuts

Tauri 2 allows you to register global shortcuts. This plugin requires a Rust version of at least 1.77.2.

### Installation

There are three general methods of installation: using crates.io and npm (easiest), pulling sources directly from GitHub using git tags/revision hashes (most secure), or Git submodule install.(no content)You can add the dependency to your Cargo.toml with `tauri-plugin-global-shortcut = "2.0.0"` and install the JavaScript bindings using `pnpm add @tauri-apps/plugin-global-shortcut`.

### Rust Implementation

In Rust, you can register shortcuts using the Builder pattern with `.with_shortcuts(["ctrl+d", "alt+space"])` and handle events by checking `shortcut.matches(Modifiers::CONTROL, Code::KeyD)` with a handler that emits events.

### JavaScript Implementation

On the frontend, you can use the register function: `await register('CommandOrControl+Shift+C', () => { console.log('Shortcut triggered'); });`

### Menu Accelerators

There's a known issue (opened April 2025) where Numpad keys can be used as global shortcuts, but not as accelerators in a MenuItem. For example, using `.accelerator("num5")` in a MenuItemBuilder compiles but fails at runtime with an error.

### Documentation for Valid Keys

Tauri features an API for defining global shortcuts via JavaScript, but users have noted a need for documentation on how to define "shortcut command strings" and where to find a list of accepted key codes/modifiers.(no content)The relevant source files for shortcut logic are located at `https://github.com/tauri-apps/tao/blob/dev/src/keyboard.rs` and `https://github.com/tauri-apps/tao/blob/dev/src/accelerator.rs`.

### Related Resources
- Official Tauri 2 Global Shortcut Plugin: https://v2.tauri.app/plugin/global-shortcut/
- JavaScript API Reference: https://v2.tauri.app/reference/javascript/global-shortcut/
- GitHub global-hotkey library: https://github.com/tauri-apps/global-hotkey

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Tauri 2 native context menus:

## Tauri 2 Native Context Menu Support

Tauri v2 has been released and it supports creating native context menu without plugins. This is a significant improvement over v1.

### Key Features in Tauri 2

Tauri 2 added `Window::popup_menu` and `Window::popup_menu_at` to show a context menu on the window at the cursor position or at a specific position.(no content)You can also popup a context menu using `popup` and `popup_at` methods from the `ContextMenu` trait which is implemented for `Menu` and `Submenu` types.

### JavaScript API

The menu API allows you to popup a menu or submenu as a context menu on the specified window. If the position is provided, it is relative to the window's top-left corner.

### Window Menu Basics

To create a base-level native window menu and attach to a window, you can create various types of menu items including basic items, check items, and separators. Each custom menu item triggers an event when clicked. Use the `on_menu_event` API to handle them.(no content)Icon support for submenus is available since Tauri 2.8.0.

### For Tauri v1 Users

If you're still on Tauri v1, there's a plugin called `tauri-plugin-context-menu` to display native context menu in Tauri v1.x, since the Tauri API v1 does not support native context menu out of the box.(no content)Bug fixes will still be implemented for this plugin, but new features will not be developed by the main maintainer.

### Useful Resources

- **Official Tauri 2 Menu Docs**: https://v2.tauri.app/reference/javascript/api/namespacemenu/
- **Window Menu Guide**: https://v2.tauri.app/learn/window-menu/
- **MUDA library** (underlying context menu library): https://github.com/tauri-apps/muda

results:
Based on the search results, here's what I found about Tauri 2 native context menus:

## Tauri 2 Native Context Menu Support

Tauri v2 has been released and it supports creating native context menu without plugins. This is a significant improvement over v1.

### Key Features in Tauri 2

Tauri 2 added `Window::popup_menu` and `Window::popup_menu_at` to show a context menu on the window at the cursor position or at a specific position.(no content)You can also popup a context menu using `popup` and `popup_at` methods from the `ContextMenu` trait which is implemented for `Menu` and `Submenu` types.

### JavaScript API

The menu API allows you to popup a menu or submenu as a context menu on the specified window. If the position is provided, it is relative to the window's top-left corner.

### Window Menu Basics

To create a base-level native window menu and attach to a window, you can create various types of menu items including basic items, check items, and separators. Each custom menu item triggers an event when clicked. Use the `on_menu_event` API to handle them.(no content)Icon support for submenus is available since Tauri 2.8.0.

### For Tauri v1 Users

If you're still on Tauri v1, there's a plugin called `tauri-plugin-context-menu` to display native context menu in Tauri v1.x, since the Tauri API v1 does not support native context menu out of the box.(no content)Bug fixes will still be implemented for this plugin, but new features will not be developed by the main maintainer.

### Useful Resources

- **Official Tauri 2 Menu Docs**: https://v2.tauri.app/reference/javascript/api/namespacemenu/
- **Window Menu Guide**: https://v2.tauri.app/learn/window-menu/
- **MUDA library** (underlying context menu library): https://github.com/tauri-apps/muda

## Implementation Phases

### Phase 1: CSS Foundation (No dependencies)
input.new_string:
## User Requirements (Confirmed)

- **Prioridade:** Todas as fases em ordem
- **Animações:** Incluir Motion (spring physics)
- **Plataforma:** macOS only
- **Foco especial:**
  - Menu bar atual tem cara de "web app" (HQ, Edit...) → precisa ser nativo macOS
  - Context menu é o default do browser → precisa ser nativo
  - DevTools pode ser inspecionado via context menu/atalho → desabilitar em produção

---

## Implementation Phases

### Phase 0: Native Menu Bar (macOS style)

**Problema atual:** O menu "HQ, Edit..." parece web, não nativo.

**File: `src-tauri/src/lib.rs`**
```rust
use tauri::menu::{Menu, Submenu, MenuItem, PredefinedMenuItem};

fn create_native_menu(app: &tauri::AppHandle) -> tauri::Result<Menu<tauri::Wry>> {
    // macOS standard menu structure
    let app_menu = Submenu::with_items(app, "HQ", true, &[
        &MenuItem::with_id(app, "about", "About HQ", true, None::<&str>)?,
        &PredefinedMenuItem::separator(app)?,
        &MenuItem::with_id(app, "settings", "Settings...", true, Some("CmdOrCtrl+,"))?,
        &PredefinedMenuItem::separator(app)?,
        &PredefinedMenuItem::services(app, None)?,
        &PredefinedMenuItem::separator(app)?,
        &PredefinedMenuItem::hide(app, None)?,
        &PredefinedMenuItem::hide_others(app, None)?,
        &PredefinedMenuItem::show_all(app, None)?,
        &PredefinedMenuItem::separator(app)?,
        &PredefinedMenuItem::quit(app, None)?,
    ])?;

    let file_menu = Submenu::with_items(app, "File", true, &[
        &MenuItem::with_id(app, "new_project", "Open Project...", true, Some("CmdOrCtrl+O"))?,
        &PredefinedMenuItem::separator(app)?,
        &MenuItem::with_id(app, "new_tab", "New Tab", true, Some("CmdOrCtrl+T"))?,
        &MenuItem::with_id(app, "close_tab", "Close Tab", true, Some("CmdOrCtrl+W"))?,
    ])?;

    let edit_menu = Submenu::with_items(app, "Edit", true, &[
        &PredefinedMenuItem::undo(app, None)?,
        &PredefinedMenuItem::redo(app, None)?,
        &PredefinedMenuItem::separator(app)?,
        &PredefinedMenuItem::cut(app, None)?,
        &PredefinedMenuItem::copy(app, None)?,
        &PredefinedMenuItem::paste(app, None)?,
        &PredefinedMenuItem::select_all(app, None)?,
    ])?;

    let view_menu = Submenu::with_items(app, "View", true, &[
        &MenuItem::with_id(app, "toggle_sidebar", "Toggle Sidebar", true, Some("CmdOrCtrl+\\"))?,
        &MenuItem::with_id(app, "toggle_logs", "Toggle Logs", true, Some("CmdOrCtrl+J"))?,
        &PredefinedMenuItem::separator(app)?,
        &PredefinedMenuItem::fullscreen(app, None)?,
    ])?;

    let window_menu = Submenu::with_items(app, "Window", true, &[
        &PredefinedMenuItem::minimize(app, None)?,
        &PredefinedMenuItem::maximize(app, None)?,
        &PredefinedMenuItem::separator(app)?,
        &MenuItem::with_id(app, "prev_tab", "Previous Tab", true, Some("CmdOrCtrl+Shift+["))?,
        &MenuItem::with_id(app, "next_tab", "Next Tab", true, Some("CmdOrCtrl+Shift+]"))?,
    ])?;

    let help_menu = Submenu::with_items(app, "Help", true, &[
        &MenuItem::with_id(app, "docs", "Documentation", true, None::<&str>)?,
        &MenuItem::with_id(app, "github", "GitHub Repository", true, None::<&str>)?,
    ])?;

    Menu::with_items(app, &[
        &app_menu,
        &file_menu,
        &edit_menu,
        &view_menu,
        &window_menu,
        &help_menu,
    ])
}
```

**Effort:** 1 hour

---

### Phase 1: CSS Foundation (No dependencies)

Here are the search results for your query about Notion context menu design and custom right-click menus in React:

## Overview

Customized context menus are commonly found on email or list applications and collaboration apps like Trello and Notion. These right-click menus (also known as context menus) give users more options while they are using the app.

## React Implementation Approaches

### Core Technique
To override the default browser right-click menu, the first step is to prevent the default right-click behavior. This can be done by listening to the contextmenu event and using the preventDefault() method on the event. Then capture the current (x,y) position, and render your component at that coordinate on the page.(no content)The critical point to note is that the custom menu should have the position style set to absolute for the menu to open at the correct position.

### React-Specific Implementation
A context menu, often referred to as a right-click menu, is a pop-up menu that appears when a user right-clicks on an element. The oncontextmenu event is typically triggered by clicking the right mouse button. In the context of React, we can utilize the onContextMenu event to capture the right-click action.

## Libraries & Tools

1. **React Flow** - The onNodeContextMenu event can be used to show a custom menu when right-clicking a node.

2. **use-context-menu** - A custom React Hook that allows you to place custom context menu(s) (right click menu) throughout your codebase.

3. **rctx-contextmenu** - There are lots of times when you want to customize the right click menu or Context Menu. You may want to add more customized options or even sub options with same look and feel how a native context menu gives. rctx-contextmenu gives that flexibility to customize Context Menu.

4. **Syncfusion React Context Menu** - React context menu or right click menu appears when users right-click or perform a touch-and-hold action. Supports multilevel nesting and templating.

5. **CRCMenu.js (July 2025)** - A lightweight JavaScript library for creating responsive, animated, customizable context menus styled with your own CSS or TailwindCSS. This library blocks the default right-click behavior and displays custom menu options with smooth animations and responsive positioning. The menu automatically detects different interaction contexts and displays relevant options accordingly.

## Best Practices

Don't forget to remove the event listeners in the componentWillUnmount method to avoid memory leaks from your component.(no content)With this approach, you can create context menus that provide specific actions and options, making your application more user-friendly and efficient.

Based on the search results, here's the information about VSCode `keybindings.json` format and structure:

## VSCode keybindings.json Format Structure

### Basic Structure
At its core, keybindings.json is a list of rules.(no content)If you have not yet made any customizations, the JSON object within this file will be empty: `[ ]`. Once you make customizations, they'll be added to this file following the format shown in this example where each command should include a value for `key`, `command`, and optionally `when`.

### Rule Attributes
A keyboard shortcut configuration in VS Code is also known as a keyboard rule. Each rule consists of the following attributes:
- **key**: describes the pressed key(s), for example Ctrl+F
- **command**: the identifier of the VS Code command to execute, for example `workbench.view.explorer` to open the Explorer view
- **when**: (optional) clause containing a boolean expression that is evaluated depending on the current context

### Example Format
```json
[
  {
    "key": "ctrl+d",
    "command": "editor.action.deleteLines",
    "when": "editorTextFocus"
  }
]
```

### How Rules Work
The additional keybindings.json rules are appended at runtime to the bottom of the default rules, thus allowing them to overwrite the default rules. The keybindings.json file is watched by VS Code, so editing it while VS Code is running will update the rules at run-time. The keyboard shortcuts dispatching is done by analyzing a list of rules that are expressed in JSON.

### Removing/Disabling Shortcuts
To remove a keyboard shortcut by using the keybindings.json file, add a `-` to the command and the rule will be a removal rule.

Example:
```json
{ "key": "tab", "command": "-jumpToNextSnippetPlaceholder" }
```

### Common "when" Clause Values
Common values for the when property include:
- **editorTextFocus**: True when the text editor has focus
- **editorLangId**: Represents the language identifier of the current file (e.g., 'typescript', 'javascript')
- **resourceExtname**: The file extension of the current resource (e.g., '.js', '.ts')
- **resourceScheme**: The URI scheme of the resource (e.g., 'file', 'untitled')

### Platform-Specific Bindings
VS Code allows you to define platform-specific keybindings directly in your keybindings.json. For example, you can override the key for a command on macOS compared to Windows or Linux using a "mac" property.

### Opening keybindings.json
Press F1 or Ctrl Shift P or Cmd Shift P to open the "Show All Commands" search box, then search for "keyboard" and choose "Preferences: Open Keyboard Shortcuts (JSON)".


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Here are the search results for Radix UI context menu and dropdown menu:

## Radix UI Dropdown Menu

"Displays a menu to the user—such as a set of actions or functions—triggered by a button."

**Key Features:**
- Supports submenus with configurable reading direction, items, labels, groups of items, and checkable items (single or multiple) with optional indeterminate state.
- Supports modal and non-modal modes, customizable side, alignment, offsets, collision handling, optionally renders a pointing arrow, and focus is fully managed.

## Radix UI Context Menu

"Displays a menu located at the pointer, triggered by a right click or a long press."

**Key Features:**
- Supports submenus with configurable reading direction, items, labels, groups of items, checkable items (single or multiple) with optional indeterminate state, and modal/non-modal modes.
- Focus is fully managed with full keyboard navigation, typeahead support, and highly customizable dismissing and layering behavior.

## Interactive Dropdown Menus with Radix UI

One tutorial shows how to build a flow from a menu to dialog with the Radix DropdownMenu and ContextMenu.(no content)A context menu is the menu that opens when you right click. When using the ContextMenu component from Radix, you are overriding the default right click menu. The APIs for ContextMenu and DropdownMenu are similar, which is great for quickly mirroring their behavior.

## NPM Package

The `@radix-ui/react-dropdown-menu` package is at version 2.1.16, last published 5 months ago. There are 4149 other projects in the npm registry using this package.

## Recent Updates

Radix has added a `radix-ui` package that exposes the latest version of all Radix Primitives from a single place. This tree-shakable entrypoint makes it easier to bring in whatever components you need and keep them up-to-date without worrying about conflicting or duplicate dependencies.

Based on my search results, here's what I found about PatternFly context menus and dropdown menus in React:

## PatternFly Dropdown Component

To provide users with more context about a `<DropdownItem>`, you can pass a short message to the `description` property.

The PatternFly documentation at [patternfly.org/components/menus/dropdown](https://www.patternfly.org/components/menus/dropdown/) covers the current dropdown implementation.

## Context Selector (Deprecated)

A context selector is a dropdown menu placed in the global navigation, which allows you to switch a user's application context to display relevant data and resources. However, the context selector component is now deprecated, and a context selector may now be built using the new suite of menu components.(no content)This is showcased in demos which use the new dropdown component that is built off of the menu component.

## PatternFly 5+ Component Architecture

Under the hood, the select component now reuses more foundational PatternFly components of Menu and MenuToggle. Similarly, the refactored dropdown allows for toggle and menu customization, and is in line with accessibility best practices.(no content)If you don't see exactly what you're looking for, you can build a custom implementation using the Menu and MenuToggle components.

## Right-Click Context Menu

For right-click context menus specifically in React, the `onNodeContextMenu` event can be used to show a custom menu when right-clicking a node. This example is from React Flow, but the same pattern applies - you would handle the `contextmenu` event and render a PatternFly Menu component at the click position.

**Note:** The search results didn't return specific documentation about PatternFly 6. The current PatternFly documentation appears to be the latest version. For implementing a right-click context menu with PatternFly, you would typically combine native browser `contextmenu` event handling with PatternFly's `Menu` and `Popper` components for positioning.

## Right-Click Context Menu

For right-click context menus specifically in React, the `onNodeContextMenu` event can be used to show a custom menu when right-clicking a node. This example is from React Flow, but the same pattern applies - you would handle the `contextmenu` event and render a PatternFly Menu component at the click position.

**Note:** The search results didn't return specific documentation about PatternFly 6. The current PatternFly documentation appears to be the latest version. For implementing a right-click context menu with PatternFly, you would typically combine native browser `contextmenu` event handling with PatternFly's `Menu` and `Popper` components for positioning.

content.content:
# PatternFly 6 Dropdown Menu Guide

## Core Components

PatternFly 6 provides a comprehensive menu system with these primary components:

- **Dropdown**: Wraps commonly used properties for dropdown menus
- **DropdownList**: Container for menu items
- **DropdownItem**: Individual selectable menu entries
- **DropdownGroup**: Groups related items with optional labels
- **MenuToggle**: The button that opens/closes the menu
- **Menu**: Base component for all menu variations

## Creating Basic Dropdowns

The fundamental structure combines a toggle with a list:

```
<Dropdown>
  <MenuToggle onClick={handleClick}>
    Actions
  </MenuToggle>
  <DropdownList>
    <DropdownItem>Option 1</DropdownItem>
    <DropdownItem>Option 2</DropdownItem>
  </DropdownList>
</Dropdown>
```

Key properties include:
- `isOpen`: Controls menu visibility
- `onOpenChange`: Callback for state changes
- `onClick`: Triggered when items are selected

## Menu Features

**Item Capabilities:**
- Disable items with `isDisabled` property
- Add descriptions below labels via `description` prop
- Separate sections using `<Divider key="separator">`
- Apply danger styling for destructive actions

**Toggle Variants:**
- Standard labeled buttons
- Kebab (three-dot) icons for space-constrained layouts
- Plain text toggles
- Split buttons with multiple actions

## Positioning & Display

Menus support directional positioning through the `popperProps` or placement modifiers. The documentation references "custom menu examples" for advanced positioning scenarios, though specific positioning syntax isn't detailed in this excerpt.

## Notable Limitation

The page mentions "context menus" only as a navigation link to topology documentation—no right-click context menu implementation details are provided here.

content.content:
# Menu Component API Reference

## Core Components

**Menu** is the primary container that manages menu state and interactions. Key props include:

- `activeItemId`: Identifies the currently active item
- `onSelect`: Callback when item selection changes
- `isScrollable`: Makes long menus scrollable and visually condensed
- `containsDrilldown`: Indicates if menu contains nested drilldown menus
- `role`: Set to `"listbox"` for non-checkbox selection menus

**MenuList** wraps menu items and accepts:
- `aria-label`: Provides accessible naming
- `isAriaMultiselectable`: For multi-select listbox menus

**MenuItem** represents individual options with these capabilities:
- `itemId`: Links to onSelect/onActionClick callbacks
- `isDisabled`, `isAriaDisabled`: Disable states
- `isDanger`: Indicates destructive actions
- `to`, `isExternalLink`: Navigation properties
- `drilldownMenu`: Nested submenu support
- `description`: Additional context text
- `hasCheckbox`, `isSelected`: Selection indicators

**MenuGroup** organizes related items with `label` and `labelHeadingLevel` props.

## Popper Integration

The `MenuContainer` component links menus with toggles and handles keyboard input. It accepts `PopperOptions` for positioning:

- `placement`: Position menu relative to trigger
- `enableFlip`: Automatically flip if boundary reached
- `distance`: Space between menu and trigger
- `onOpenChange`: Callback for open/close state

"Additional properties to pass to the Popper" enable custom positioning behaviors.

## Keyboard Navigation

MenuContainer supports arrow key navigation—pressing up/down arrows focuses valid menu items. The `onToggleKeydown` callback allows overriding default behavior.

## Advanced Features

**Drilldown menus** use `drilldownItemPath`, `drilledInMenus`, and `onDrillIn`/`onDrillOut` callbacks for nested navigation. **Search filtering** integrates `MenuSearch` and `MenuSearchInput` for dynamic item filtering.

- `to`, `isExternalLink`: Navigation properties

- `description`: Additional context text
- `hasCheckbox`, `isSelected`: Selection indicators

**MenuGroup** organizes related items with `label` and `labelHeadingLevel` props.

## Popper Integration

The `MenuContainer` component links menus with toggles and handles keyboard input. It accepts `PopperOptions` for positioning:

lines: +**Design:** User-configurable keyboard shortcuts via JSON file, exactly like VSCode's `keybindings.json`.

+  args?: unknown;        // Optional arguments for command
+// - cmd/ctrl → platform-aware (Cmd on macOS, Ctrl on Windows/Linux)
+// - Single keys: a-z, 0-9, f1-f12, escape, enter, tab, space, etc.
+    // Load user overrides from localStorage or file
+**User keybindings file:** `~/.config/hq/keybindings.json` (or via Tauri app data)
+- Same format as VSCode (familiar to developers)
+- Hot-reload when file changes (via file watcher)

## Implementation Order (Recommended)

- [Tauri window-vibrancy](https://github.com/tauri-apps/window-vibrancy)
- [tauri-plugin-prevent-default](https://github.com/ferreira-tb/tauri-plugin-prevent-default)
- [Making Electron Apps Feel Native](https://getlotus.app/21-making-electron-apps-feel-native-on-mac)
- [Motion (Framer Motion)](https://motion.dev/)

lines: -| `src-tauri/src/lib.rs` | Setup vibrancy, prevent-default plugin, window events |
+| `src-tauri/src/lib.rs` | Native menu bar, vibrancy, prevent-default, window events |
+| `src/index.css` | Native-feel CSS (selection, focus, drag regions, cursor) |
-1. **Phase 1: CSS Foundation** - Quick wins, no dependencies
-2. **Phase 2: Disable Browser Behaviors** - Essential for native feel
-3. **Phase 3: Window Vibrancy** - Visual impact, macOS only
+1. **Phase 0: Native Menu Bar** - macOS-style app menu (critical for native feel)
+2. **Phase 1: CSS Foundation** - Quick wins, no dependencies
+3. **Phase 2: Disable Browser Behaviors** - Essential (no DevTools inspect, no Cmd+F)
+4. **Phase 3: Window Vibrancy** - Visual impact, macOS blur
+7. **Phase 6: Animations** - Motion library for spring physics
+8. **Phase 7: Focus/Blur State** - Dim UI when window unfocused
1. **Vibrancy is macOS-only** - Windows/Linux will have solid background
-2. **backdrop-filter bug in Tauri 2.2.5** - CSS blur may not work with transparency enabled
-3. **Linux pinch-zoom** - Cannot be fully disabled (GTK limitation)
-4. **NumPad accelerators** - Don't work in menu items (Tauri bug)
+2. **backdrop-filter bug in Tauri 2.2.5** - CSS blur may conflict with window transparency
+3. **DevTools in dev mode** - Keep enabled for debugging, disable only in production
+- [ ] Menu bar looks native macOS (HQ, File, Edit, View, Window, Help)
- [ ] No focus outlines on mouse click, only on Tab navigation
- [ ] Text not selectable on buttons, labels, headers
+- [ ] Cmd+F, Cmd+P, right-click "Inspect" disabled in production
+- [ ] DevTools accessible in dev mode only
+- [ ] Window has translucent blur effect (macOS vibrancy)
+- [ ] Right-click shows custom PatternFly context menu (Notion-style)
+- [ ] Keyboard shortcuts configurable via JSON file
- [ ] UI dims when window loses focus

content.thinking: O plano está completo. Agora vou resumir o que foi feito e perguntar ao usuário se há mais alguma coisa que ele quer adicionar ou se posso sair do modo de planejamento.

content.text:
Plano completo! Resumo do que foi pesquisado e planejado:

## Resumo da Pesquisa F012: Native UX Polish

### Componentes já disponíveis (não precisamos adicionar):
- **PatternFly 6** → Menu, MenuList, MenuItem, Popper (para context menus)

### Dependências a adicionar:
- `window-vibrancy` (Rust) → blur macOS
- `tauri-plugin-prevent-default` (Rust) → bloquear Cmd+F, DevTools, etc.
- `motion` (npm) → animações spring

### 8 Fases (~9 horas):
| # | Fase | Descrição |
|---|------|-----------|
| 0 | Native Menu Bar | Menu macOS padrão (HQ, File, Edit, View, Window, Help) |
| 1 | CSS Foundation | focus-visible, user-select, cursor, font-size 14px |
| 2 | Prevent Default | Bloquear Cmd+F, DevTools (prod), zoom, context menu browser |
| 3 | Vibrancy | Blur translúcido macOS Tahoe |
| 4 | Context Menus | PatternFly Menu no right-click (Notion-style) |
| 5 | Keybindings | JSON configurável (VSCode-style) |
| 6 | Animations | Motion library (spring physics) |
| 7 | Focus/Blur | Dim UI quando janela perde foco |
